principle.name: Mobile-First But Not Mobile-Only
principle.description: Every interface must work beautifully on phones, tablets, laptops, and large desktop screens. Start with mobile constraints and expand upward, not the reverse.
principle.rationale: Mobile constraints force prioritization. Desktop-first designs often feel bloated on mobile.

principle.name: Responsive By Structure, Not By Accident
principle.description: Layouts must adapt intentionally with clear behavior for each breakpoint. Define explicit breakpoints: 320px (small phone), 375px (phone), 768px (tablet), 1024px (tablet landscape), 1280px (desktop), 1536px (large desktop).
principle.rationale: Ad-hoc responsive design creates edge cases. Structured responsive design is predictable.

principle.name: Touch-First Where Needed
principle.description: Any feature that may be used on mobile or tablet must account for: tap, double-tap, long-press, drag, swipe, pinch-to-zoom, two-finger pan, gesture conflicts, accidental touch prevention, large enough hit targets, thumb-friendly reach.
principle.rationale: Touch interactions have different ergonomics than mouse. Ignoring them creates poor mobile experiences.

principle.name: Desktop Interactions Must Remain Efficient
principle.description: For desktop users, support: hover states when useful, right-click/context menus, keyboard shortcuts where appropriate, precise cursor interactions, high-density workflows, multi-panel productivity layouts.
principle.rationale: Desktop users have different expectations for speed and density than mobile users.

principle.name: Browser-Native UX
principle.description: Design with browser constraints and opportunities: scroll behavior, overflow handling, sticky areas, modals, side panels, pointer events, touch-action behavior, focus states, PWA-friendly patterns, responsive viewport issues.
principle.rationale: Browser-native patterns feel intuitive because they match user expectations built from years of browsing.

principle.name: Implementation-Aware Design
principle.description: Always think about how this will be built in React/Next.js: component reusability, state complexity, gesture handling, accessibility, rendering performance, scaling the design system over time.
principle.rationale: Designs that ignore implementation realities often fail in production or require expensive redesigns.

library.name: shadcn/ui

use_when.scenario: The feature benefits from faster assembly
A polished default visual system is needed
Reusable app-level components with minimal setup are desired
Building on top of Radix primitives with Tailwind customization

library.reasoning: shadcn/ui provides copy-paste component code, not a library dependency. It offers polished defaults while remaining fully customizable. Best for application UIs that need to match a specific design language quickly.

library.name: Radix UI

use_when.scenario: Highly specialized interaction behavior is required

library.reasoning: Radix UI provides unstyled, accessible primitives that give full control over rendering. Best for design systems or UIs with unique interaction patterns that shadcn/ui components cannot achieve without significant overrides.

library.name: Custom Components

use_when.scenario: Neither shadcn/ui nor Radix provides a suitable base
The interaction pattern is highly specialized (canvas, maps, whiteboard)
Performance requirements demand complete control over rendering

library.reasoning: Custom components are necessary for canvas-based editors, map interfaces, drag-and-drop systems, and other specialized interactions. However, they require more development time and maintenance.

guideline.name: hit_targets
guideline.spacing: At least 8px between touch targets to prevent accidental activation
guideline.thumb_zone: Primary actions should be in the bottom third of the screen for one-handed use

guideline.name: gesture_conflicts
guideline.issue: Gestures must not conflict with browser gestures (scroll, zoom, navigation)
guideline.solution: Use distinct gesture contexts or provide explicit mode switching

example.solution: Disable browser zoom when canvas is active, provide explicit UI for zoom

guideline.name: visual_feedback
guideline.requirement: All touch interactions must provide immediate visual feedback
guideline.techniques: Ripple effects, scale transforms, opacity changes, loading indicators

pattern.name: mobile_stacked
pattern.description: Single column layout, bottom navigation, collapsible sections

pattern.name: tablet_transitional
pattern.description: Two-column where useful, side panel can appear, larger touch targets

pattern.name: multi_panel_desktop
pattern.description: Persistent sidebars, inspector panels, toolbars, high-density layouts
pattern.use_when: Desktop and large tablets in landscape

pattern.name: adaptive_sidebar
pattern.description: Sidebar collapses to icons on smaller screens, expands on hover/click on larger

pattern.name: bottom_bar_mobile
pattern.description: Primary navigation at bottom for thumb accessibility

guideline.name: single_responsibility
guideline.rule: Each component should do one thing well

example.bad: Button that also handles loading state and error display
example.good: Button component + ButtonLoading wrapper + ButtonError variant

guideline.name: composition_over_configuration
guideline.rule: Prefer composable components over highly configurable single components

guideline.name: layout_components
guideline.rule: Create layout components for recurring structures

examples.component: Sidebar, Header, Footer for page structure

pitfall.description: Designing only for the happy path
pitfall.why_problematic: Users encounter errors, empty states, loading states, and edge cases
pitfall.correct_approach: Design for empty, loading, error, and success states equally

pitfall.why_problematic: Hover, right-click, and precise cursor interactions don't exist on touch
pitfall.correct_approach: Design touch alternatives for all critical interactions

pitfall.why_problematic: Users cannot discover or remember complex gestures
pitfall.correct_approach: Limit to common gestures; provide explicit UI alternatives

pitfall.description: Ignoring performance implications of complex UI
pitfall.why_problematic: Animations, large lists, and complex states can cause jank
pitfall.correct_approach: Consider rendering performance, use virtualization for lists, optimize animations

pitfall.why_problematic: Excludes users with disabilities; may violate legal requirements
pitfall.correct_approach: Start with accessible patterns, then enhance for those who can use advanced features

category.name: before_starting
category.item: Understand the full scope of the request
Identify all target platforms and breakpoints
Review existing design patterns in the codebase
Check technology constraints (React version, CSS capabilities)

category.name: during_design
category.item: Specify touch interactions for all interactive elements
Make shadcn/ui vs Radix UI decision explicit
Consider state variations (empty, loading, error, success)

category.name: before_completion
category.item: Verify all 9 output sections are addressed
Review gesture conflicts and edge cases
